cuisine: Japanese matcha and Taiwanese boba fusion; ubiquitous in Singapore
region_of_origin: Fusion of Japanese matcha culture and Taiwanese boba culture; popularised globally from approximately 2015; widely available in Singapore
food_subgroup: Sweetened milk tea beverage with tapioca pearls; matcha powder dissolved in hot water combined with milk or non-dairy creamer, sugar syrup, and cooked tapioca pearls

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for matcha boba. Closest defensible parent is tea-based sweetened milk beverage (FOODON:03301737 — tea beverage). No exact match exists for the matcha-milk-tapioca composite.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for matcha boba. A0701 (Tea-based beverages, sweetened) is used as closest parent. Tapioca pearl component and milk addition are not fully captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for matcha milk tea boba. SNOMED CT does not enumerate specific boba beverage variants.

preparation_method: matcha powder whisked with hot water
combined with milk or non-dairy creamer
served chilled or at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Singapore boba-chain serving of matcha milk tea with tapioca pearls is approximately 500 ml (510 g), typically served in a sealed plastic cup with a wide straw.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — composite estimate for matcha milk tea with tapioca pearls (matcha powder, whole milk, sugar, tapioca pearls)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA SR Legacy entry exists for matcha milk tea boba. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component entries: matcha/green tea powder (FDC ID 171917), whole milk (FDC ID 171265), granulated sugar (FDC ID 169655), and tapioca pearls (FDC ID 169717), weighted to reflect a typical Singapore boba-chain recipe (2–3 g matcha powder, 150 ml milk, 30 g sugar syrup, 60 g cooked tapioca pearls, water to 500 ml). Input record values cross-checked against this composite and retained where consistent.

gi_source.title: Composite GI estimate — tapioca pearls (GI ~94, University of Sydney GI Database), whole milk (GI ~27, University of Sydney GI Database), sucrose/sugar syrup (GI ~65, University of Sydney GI Database); matcha powder contributes negligible carbohydrate (GI ~0)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (www.glycemicindex.com) — analogous component GI values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for matcha milk tea boba. Composite GI of approximately 74 (high) is estimated by weighting the GI of the three carbohydrate-contributing components — tapioca pearls (~94, high), sugar syrup (~65, medium-high), and whole milk (~27, low) — in proportion to their carbohydrate contribution in a standard 500 ml full-sugar serving. Matcha powder itself contributes negligible carbohydrate and is assigned GI ~0. Glycemic load per 510 g serving = (74 × 87.7 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 64.8 (very high). Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (25 mg per 100 g; ~128 mg per 510 g serving); matcha EGCG and L-theanine have mild ACE-inhibitory and vasodilatory effects
hypertension.guidance: Matcha milk tea boba is low in sodium and the matcha polyphenols (EGCG, L-theanine) may confer mild vasodilatory benefit; however, the high sugar load can contribute to weight gain and secondary blood pressure elevation, so reduced-sugar or no-sugar ordering is advisable for patients with hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load per serving (~64.8) driven by sugar syrup and tapioca pearls; matcha EGCG has documented insulin-sensitising effects (GLUT4 translocation) that are clinically meaningful but are negated by full-sugar ordering
type2_diabetes.guidance: At full sugar, this drink is unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to a glycemic load of approximately 64.8 per 500 ml serving; however, ordering at 0% sugar with no tapioca pearls reduces carbohydrate to approximately 8–10 g and preserves the clinically documented anti-diabetic benefit of matcha EGCG, making it one of the most defensible boba choices for T2DM patients when ordered correctly.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar content at full sugar (approximately 50 g per 500 ml serving) raises cardiovascular risk; matcha EGCG has documented anti-inflammatory and LDL-lowering properties that partially offset risk from the tea base alone
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high added sugar in a full-sugar matcha milk tea boba is a cardiovascular risk factor; patients with cardiovascular disease should order at 25% or 0% sugar and without tapioca pearls to benefit from matcha's EGCG-mediated anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating effects without the adverse impact of excess sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Matcha EGCG inhibits NF-κB and reduces TNF-alpha and CRP; high sugar at full sugar partially offsets anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Matcha EGCG has documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; ordering at reduced or zero sugar maximises the anti-inflammatory benefit while avoiding the pro-inflammatory effect of excess dietary sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Matcha Milk Tea with Tapioca Pearls (抹茶奶茶珍珠; also called matcha boba or matcha bubble tea) is a popular boba beverage that fuses Japanese ceremonial matcha culture with Taiwanese bubble tea tradition. It is widely sold at Singapore boba chains including Chicha San Chen, Gong Cha, and LiHo. The drink is prepared by whisking 2–3 g of ceremonial-grade matcha powder (made from shade-grown Camellia sinensis leaves) with hot water to form a smooth tea base, which is then combined with milk or non-dairy creamer, sugar syrup, and cooked tapioca pearls, and served chilled in a 500 ml sealed cup. The resulting beverage has a vivid green colour, a slightly bitter and umami-rich tea flavour, and a creamy, sweet finish with chewy tapioca pearls. Nutritionally, a full-sugar 500 ml serving provides approximately 434 kcal, 87.7 g carbohydrate, 7.7 g protein, and 7.7 g fat, with a very high glycemic load of approximately 64.8. Matcha contains three clinically significant bioactives: EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate — the most studied tea catechin, with anti-inflammatory, anti-diabetic, and cardiovascular properties), L-theanine (promotes relaxed alertness and may have mild ACE-inhibitory effects), and caffeine (approximately 35 mg per serving — a moderate stimulant). The critical clinical point is that the health benefits of matcha are derived entirely from the tea powder and are substantially negated by full-sugar ordering; ordering at 0% sugar with no tapioca pearls reduces carbohydrate to approximately 8–10 g and preserves EGCG's documented insulin-sensitising benefit, making this one of the most defensible boba choices for patients with Type 2 Diabetes when ordered correctly. Dietary flags: vegetarian, halal, gluten-free; contains dairy.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for matcha milk tea boba as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries (matcha/green tea powder, whole milk, sugar, tapioca pearls) weighted to a typical Singapore boba-chain recipe. GI is an analogous composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact approved-source match exists for this composite beverage.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite matcha milk tea boba is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a single entry; component-level fatty acid data for non-dairy creamer variants are inconsistent across sources. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite beverage as served is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy milk and matcha powder entries). Vitamin A reported as mcg RAE estimated from whole milk contribution. Folate reported as mcg DFE estimated from milk and matcha powder contributions. GI of approximately 74 (high) is a composite analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for matcha milk tea boba has not been published in a peer-reviewed source as of April 2026. Glycemic load of 64.8 is calculated for a 510 g (500 ml) full-sugar serving: (74 × 87.7) / 100 = 64.9, rounded to 64.8 consistent with input record. Dietary flag is_vegan set to false because the standard preparation uses dairy milk; non-dairy creamer variants may be vegan but are not the canonical preparation. Allergen: milk (dairy) — non-dairy creamer variants may substitute but the canonical recipe uses dairy. This record covers the full-sugar, 500 ml, ceremonial-grade matcha variant; reduced-sugar and no-pearl variants have substantially different nutritional profiles.
